title: podman rootless container w/ passed devices reconnecting, results in permission 0000
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I am running a rootless container, and passing `--device /dev/vide0` (a v4l device) to `podman run`, the container launches fine (presuming the usb-webcam is plugged in and /dev/vide0 exists), however after some time, if the webcam gets disconnected, /dev/vide0 naturally dissappears on the host.

The issue happens when it's plugged back in, /dev/vide0 re-appears on host as before, however _inside_ the container on /dev/vide0 now shows permission 0000:

```
bash-5.0# ls -l /dev/video*
c---------    0 nobody   nobody     81,   0 Mar 25 13:17 /dev/video0
bash-5.0# chmod 666 /dev/video0
chmod: /dev/video0: Operation not permitted
```

whereas outside the container:

```
crw-rw-rw- 1 root video 81, 0 Mar 26 14:43 /dev/video0
```

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. plug in webcam

2.start container with `--device /dev/video0`

3.after container is running, unplug/replug device and verify permissions inside container

**Describe the results you received:**

permissions are nulled, all 0's.. chmod'ing the device results in operation not permitted (likely since we're running rootless)

**Describe the results you expected:**

permissions should be set to as they were on the host

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.8.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.11.6
OS/Arch:            linux/arm
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

  compiler: gc
  git commit: ""   
  go version: go1.11.6
  podman version: 1.8.2
host:
  BuildahVersion: 1.14.3

  Conmon:
    package: 'conmon: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon'
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.14, commit: '
  Distribution:
    distribution: raspbian
    version: "10"
  IDMappings:
    gidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
    uidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
  MemFree: 872448
  MemTotal: 237453312
  OCIRuntime:
    name: runc
    package: 'cri-o-runc: /usr/lib/cri-o-runc/sbin/runc'
    path: /usr/lib/cri-o-runc/sbin/runc
    version: 'runc version spec: 1.0.1-dev'
  SwapFree: 209186816
  SwapTotal: 234876928
  arch: arm
  cpus: 1
  eventlogger: journald
  hostname: raspberrypi
  kernel: 4.19.97+
  os: linux
  rootless: true
  slirp4netns:
    Executable: /usr/bin/slirp4netns
    Package: 'slirp4netns: /usr/bin/slirp4netns'
    Version: |-
      slirp4netns version 0.4.3
      commit: unknown
  uptime: 25h 49m 15.81s (Approximately 1.04 days)

  search:
  - docker.io

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
$ apt-cache show podman
Package: podman
Version: 1.8.2~1

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
@mheon any ideas? 

as a workaround for another device file, i passed (as a volume mount) the whole of `/dev/` into the container as `/host_dev`, however for _this_ device file, v4l won't accept it if it's not exactly /dev/video0 (still investigating why), so am forced to use `--device` (same issue however if i simply use `-v` instead of `--device`), and the workaround cannot be used so still is useful to have someone figure out if there's a way to have permissions corrected on re-attach of the device?

Are you running root, or rootless? And whichever you are running, can you try the other?

Reasoning: we use different methods for devices as root (we actually make a device node with the major/minor number of the device on the host) and rootless (no permissions for that, so we just make a bind mount). I'd be interested to see if either of those works.

was running as rootless.. i tried this as root and the device stays visible with same perms, i guess this is the use case that you use mknod(2) on.. and rootless is the bind mount (where permissions disppaear and don't get re-set if/when the device re-appears).

what are your thoughts on fixing this then?  

for the record, i was also able to work around this issue of permissions not being restored, w/ my particular app at least..  seems the v4l-using app had a [limited size](https://github.com/jacksonliam/mjpg-streamer/blob/master/mjpg-streamer-experimental/plugins/input_uvc/v4l2uvc.c#L105) (16-byte) buffer for holding the path, that i was previously exceeding inadvertently.. thankfully shortening the path where i mount /dev inside the container works around the issue.

@giuseppe This is another version of the same issue we had before where devices are coming and going that were mapped into the container.

A couple of ideas, and possible solution.

1. Could we do something with conmon to watch for this situation and have it fix the issue?
2. Could we do something with fuse-overlay to watch for this situation and fix it?
3. We could do something with Podman but that would not work with `-d` flags.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@giuseppe Any further thoughts on this one?

I'd suggest to try overriding `/dev` inside of the container.  That is the solution we were evaluating for solving a similar issue.

You can use -v `/dev/:/dev:rslave` so the entire dev is bind mounted instead of creating bind mounts for each device.  Have you already tried it?  Do you hit any problem if you bind mount the entire `/dev`?
@giuseppe Did you ever work further on this?
I am still waiting for more feedback on my last suggestion.
So the risk would be on tty devices owned by the user.  But we already have this.
@aleks-mariusz @giuseppe needs feedback.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I have a similar issue trying to mount a usb (tty) device into a rootless container.  Is there still work being done on this?
You could just mount /dev/ into the container?
@giuseppe Is this something we could handle with bpf and conmon?

The issue that I can't figure out how to solve with mounting /dev into the container is permissions / groups.  
``` shell
[host]$ ls -lat /dev/ttyACM0
crw-rw----. 1 root dialout 166, 0 Nov  6 17:03 /dev/ttyACM0
[host]$ podman run  --rm -ti --userns=keep-id  --annotation io.crun.keep_original_groups=1 -v /dev/:/hostdev/:rslave fedora bash
[container]$ ls -lat /hostdev/ttyACM0
crw-rw----. 1 nobody nobody 166, 0 Nov  6 22:03 /hostdev/ttyACM0
[container]$ cat /hostdev/ttyACM0
cat: /hostdev/ttyACM0: Permission denied
```
If using crun try: `--annotation run.oci.keep_original_groups=1`

@giuseppe I think we should make this a full option in podman --keep-gid, then set this magical annotation under the covers.
Report error if runc does not support it.

~as you can see above, I was using the new annotation.~  It appears I add the annotation wrong?  Perhaps it changed?  I tried `--annotation run.oci.keep_original_groups=1` and still permission denied.

versions 

``` shell
$ cat /etc/redhat-release
Fedora release 32 (Thirty Two)
$ podman --version
podman version 2.0.5
$ crun --version
crun version 0.14.1
commit: 598ea5e192ca12d4f6378217d3ab1415efeddefa
spec: 1.0.0
+SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +YAJL```
Is it SELinux?
This bug seems to hit the nail on the head, if I disable label separation it seems to work. https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1770553
The issue is SELinux is not allowed to open a random TTY on the host.
any better options than `--security-opt label=disable`?

We could write customer policy for the container.  Perhaps we could look at udica for it.  @wrabcak wdyt?  Basically generate a container type which has full access to devices. Only tty devices would be better.

recent podman releases have a new `--mount type=devpts` to mount a new devpts on top on `/dev`.

Does `--mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts` make any difference with the issue above?

I'm not sure if this is what you are asking for, but I still do get permission denied when running the below

```
$ podman run  --rm -ti --userns=keep-id  --annotation run.oci.keep_original_groups=1 --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts -v /dev/:/hostdev/:rslave fedora bash

[container]$ cat /hostdev/ttyACM0
cat: hostdev/ttyACM0: Permission denied
```

Hi All, 
@rhatdan  is there a difference by using '-v' for device and using 
'--device' ? 

It will work if -v switch is used, for --device we need to add the functionality for udica.

--device needs no special labelling, this just means that the device will get injected into the container, with the correct label.

-v /dev:/dev is the case where users get the permission denied.
I see. Okay I create draft PR for that functionality in Udica: https://github.com/containers/udica/pull/78
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

does the issue still exist with ` -v /dev/:/dev:rslave --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts`?

I think we should document this behavior, but not make it the default as we expose all devices to the container, even if unprivileged
SELinux will also block some of the access to devices which could cause spurious AVCs.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Is there any way to workaorund, and map the device's ownership into the container? As the device files come in as nobody:nogroup, I presume a call to open them results in success, but I am running a CUPS server, which won't see my printer in the admin page if it's marked as being owned by nobody. Currently my workaround is just to make a new device node at boot and chage the ownership explicitly to my unpriveliged container user, which isn't great.

Obviously the device files are in a different namespace to the container, but it seems a bit unintuitive to me that they should then appear as nobody:nogroup on the inside. In this case, I have access to `lp` as my host user, the container has access to files owned by `lp` as root, but then fails because it can't see itself as having access to those files. This makes a bit more sense for files, but feels to me like it's circumventing the group mechanisms a bit. And using stat to discover more about files is pretty common - a number of applications will use this to check permissions before opening a file rather than just trying and getting lucky.

Is there any way that `run.oci.keep_original_groups` could map groups that have permissions outside the container into some virtual group inside that could be managed? Again, for 1:1 mappings I think the existing situation makes sense, that the running user -> root and anything else -> nobody, but would it be possible to map the groups that the runtime inherits into the root group?  In this sense it would replicate the behaviour, that if in host-land I am a member of the group that owns a file and has write perms, I can write it. If these become the root group inside, then this corresponds to root's being able to write the file container-side.

If an user doesn't have privileges to use a device on the host, then it won't in the rootless user namespace as well.

Devices owned by root on the host will appear as `nobody:nogroup` in the rootless container.  There is no way to circumvent it.

All you can do is to make sure the device is either world writeable/readable or owned by a group that the unprivileged user is part of.

I've not got any feedback on my suggestion of using `-v /dev/:/dev:rslave --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts` so I am going to close the issue.  Please reopen if it still doesn't work.

> SELinux will also block some of the access to devices which could cause spurious AVCs.

for rootless containers we will inherit the SELinux label in any case since we do a bind mount, but the override for `devpts` should be enough to not mess with the ttys on the host
